question_title: Will the interest in “dayton james webber” change between 2026-03-30 and 2026-04-07 according to Google Trends?
summary: Dayton James Webber (quadruple amputee cornhole player charged with murder) spiked to Google Trends value 100 on March 24, then decayed rapidly to 1 by March 29-30. At baseline value 1, "Decreases" is mathematically impossible (would require value < -2, but GT minimum is 0). "Increases" requires value > 4, needing a new catalyst. No court hearing is scheduled within the forecast window (next hearing April 10+). The "Doesn't change" band of [0,4] captures all plausible baseline noise. Small probability reserved for "Increases" in case of unexpected viral development (new evidence, interview, etc.).

factors.description: Current value is 1 (baseline floor), making 'Decreases' (requires <-2) mathematically impossible since GT minimum is 0
Post-spike decay pattern: 100→23→8→4→2→1 over 6 days. Interest has returned to pre-spike baseline. Textbook exponential decay already completed.
Change stats from resolution window show 77.3% no-change rate (17/22 transitions within ±3). Stable tail of 3 days at range [1,4] confirms plateau at baseline.
No scheduled court hearing within the forecast window (next hearing April 10 or 23, both after April 7 resolution). No identified catalyst for renewed search interest.
The story is still being covered by major outlets (TMZ, NY Post, ESPN) as of March 28-29 with secondary angles. Small chance of a new viral development causing a mini-spike above 4.
SerpAPI vs pytrends measurement uncertainty at low values (0-4) could produce ±1-2 point differences, but unlikely to cross the ±3 threshold from baseline

reasoning:
**Forecast: "Doesn't change" at 88%**

Key reasoning:
- **Current value: 1** (baseline floor after a spike that peaked at 100 on March 24)
- **"Decreases" is impossible**: At value 1, even dropping to 0 is only -1, well within the ±3 threshold
- **"Increases" needs a catalyst**: Requires value >4 on April 7, but no court hearing or scheduled event falls within the forecast window (next hearing is April 10+)
- **The spike has fully decayed**: 100→23→8→4→2→1 over 6 days — classic post-viral news decay, already back to baseline
- **"Doesn't change" band [0,4]** captures all plausible baseline noise at the floor level

The 10% on "Increases" accounts for the possibility of an unexpected viral development (new evidence, jail interview, related incident) in this high-profile case that's still getting some secondary media coverage.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves “Doesn't change” if the value on the timeline at [https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=dayton%20james%20webber&date=2026-03-08%202026-04-07](https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=dayton%20james%20webber&date=2026-03-08%202026-04-07) for 2026-04-07 is within 3 of the value at 2026-03-30. It resolves “Increases” if the value is more than 3 greater, and resolves “Decreases” if the value is more than 3 lower.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will access the data on Google Trends using [SerpApi](https://serpapi.com/), and compare the height of the timeline for the two aforementioned dates. The specific python query will be structured as follows:
```
from serpapi import GoogleSearch

search = GoogleSearch(params={
  "api_key": API_KEY,
  "engine": "google_trends",
  "data_type": "TIMESERIES",
  "geo": "US",
  "tz": 0,
  "q": "dayton james webber",
  "date": "2026-03-08 2026-04-07",
})
search.get_dict()
```
Note that there may be differences between the results returned by the API and the data appearing on the page. This seems to be due to the 'tz' parameter not having the intended effect in-browser. In this case, the API results will be considered authoritative.
Note that the precision of the timeline will be 1 day, so this will compare the overall interest for the whole day as determined by Google Trends.
Dates are determined in UTC.
If the data is no longer available, or the script fails, this question will be annulled or manually resolved by a moderator.
